source.title: Directed evolution of a filamentous fungus for thermotolerance

initial_request.research_problem: M. anisopliae has low thermotolerance (upper limit ~32°C), limiting its use as a biocontrol agent in hot climates and against insects exhibiting behavioral fever.
initial_request.known_context: Most strains display little to no growth between 35–37°C.
constraints: No a priori knowledge of required mutations
quantifiable_goals: Select for variants displaying robust growth at 37°C

evolution_trajectory.state_before: M. anisopliae has low thermotolerance, limiting biocontrol efficacy.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown genetic basis for thermotolerance; traditional engineering approaches are impractical.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Directed evolution via the Evolugator™ continuous culture system can select for thermotolerant variants without a priori knowledge of required mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use the Evolugator™ to apply gradual temperature increase as a selective pressure.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The Evolugator™ takes advantage of a natural selection-adaptation strategy.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to design the Evolugator™ setup for filamentous fungus.

evidence.quote_or_span: we used a newly developed automated continuous culture method called the Evolugator™, which takes advantage of a natural selection-adaptation strategy, to select for thermotolerant variants of M. anisopliae strain 2575 displaying robust growth at 37°C

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Evolugator™ setup ready; wild-type M. anisopliae strain 2575 inoculated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown if thermotolerant variants can arise and be selected under gradually increasing temperature.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Gradually increasing temperature over many cycles will select for variants with improved growth at higher temperatures.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Start culture at 28°C and increase temperature over 22 cycles across 4 months.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 22 successive selection cycles were performed over a 4 month period.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Monitor growth and fitness during temperature increase.

evidence.quote_or_span: Figure 1 presents a detailed description of 22 successive selection cycles over a 4 month period
Results: Over a 4 month time course, 22 cycles of growth and dilution were used to select 2 thermotolerant variants of M.

we used a newly developed automated continuous culture method called the Evolugator™, which takes advantage of a natural selection-adaptation strategy, to select for thermotolerant variants of M. anisopliae strain 2575 displaying robust growth at 37°C

However, when insects infected at 36°C were subsequently placed at 28°C, the hosts were rapidly killed by all three fungal strains, indicating that the wild type and adapted strains remained viable at 36°C, but could not cause pathogenicity and death.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Temperature increasing; growth monitored via OD.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Fitness may decline at certain temperatures, requiring strategy adjustment.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: If growth slows and OD drops, temperature should be stabilized to allow fit variants to dominate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Maximal cell yield (OD) dropped significantly during cycles 7 (31°C) and 8 (32°C), indicating decreased overall fitness. Temperature was not varied significantly in cycles 8 and 9 to allow variants to take over.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Continue selection with temperature stabilization as needed.

evidence.quote_or_span: Moreover, the maximal cell yield (OD) dropped significantly during cycles 7 ( $31^{\circ}$ C) and 8 ( $32^{\circ}$ C), even though cells were allowed to grow for over 200 hours each time, indicating decreased overall fitness.
In cycles 8 and 9, the chamber temperature was not varied significantly in order to allow variants that can grow rapidly at this temperature to take over the population
In cycles 8 and 9, the chamber temperature was not varied significantly in order to allow variants that can grow rapidly at this temperature to take over the population.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Selection ongoing; temperature increased further with similar stabilization at later barriers.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to isolate individual variants for characterization.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Variants isolated from later cycles will exhibit enhanced thermotolerance.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Two strains, EVG016 (cycle 18) and EVG017 (cycle 22), were isolated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Confirm that isolates are derivatives of wild type.

evidence.quote_or_span: Two strains, termed EVG016 and EVG017 were isolated from cells cultured in cycles 18 and 22, respectively

evolution_trajectory.state_before: EVG016 and EVG017 isolated; need to verify they are derived from the original strain.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sequencing of ITS1 and Pr1 genes will confirm identity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Both isolates were derivatives of the original wild type strain.

evidence.quote_or_span: Sequencing of the ITS1 and a fragment of the M. anisopliae specific protease Pr1 genes revealed that both isolates were derivatives of the original wild type strain
Two strains, termed EVG016 and EVG017 were isolated from cells cultured in cycles 18 and 22, respectively

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to determine upper growth temperature limits.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Growth curves at 36.5°C, 37°C, and 38°C will reveal thermotolerance limits.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure growth using Bioscreen C plate reader.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Both variants grew at 36.5°C; only EVG017 grew at 37°C; no growth at 38°C.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess sporulation and virulence for biocontrol potential.

evidence.quote_or_span: Growth curves at 36.5°C (A) and 37°C (B) of wild-type and temperature adapted M. anisopliae isolates.

Growth experiments were performed using a high-throughput Bioscreen C plate reader and are the averages of at least 30 independent growth curves each for three independent batches of fungal conidia (>90 cultures).
Both variants displayed robust growth at 36.5°C, whereas only one was able to grow at 37°C.

Due to the reduced sporulation of EVG017, not enough spores could be directly harvested for insect bioassays. Therefore, the strain was passaged once through M. sanguinipes by rubbing the abdomen of host insects on an agar culture of EVG017.

Remarkably, the resulting variant, EVG017g, maintained thermotolerance after passage through the insect and showed increased virulence compared to the non-insect passaged parent strain as measured by $ST_{50}$ .
Similar phenomena, where cycle duration needed to be increased and temperature stabilized to allow fast growing variants to take over, were also seen in cycles 16 (34.6°C) and 20 (38°C).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: EVG017 shows best thermotolerance but sporulation unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: EVG017 sporulation may be impaired, hindering bioassays.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: EVG017 may have reduced sporulation as a trade-off.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Attempt to harvest spores; if insufficient, passage through insect host.
evolution_trajectory.observation: EVG017 had severely reduced sporulation (<1% of wild type), preventing direct insect bioassay.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Passage through insect host to restore sporulation.

evidence.quote_or_span: Due to the reduced sporulation of EVG017, not enough spores could be directly harvested for insect bioassays. Therefore, the strain was passaged once through M. sanguinipes by rubbing the abdomen of host insects on an agar culture of EVG017.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to restore conidiation without losing thermotolerance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Passage through the insect host M. sanguinipes will restore sporulation while retaining thermotolerance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Passage EVG017 once through M. sanguinipes and re-isolate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Resulting variant EVG017g retained thermotolerance and showed restored conidiation (1.61 × 10^12 conidia/Kg barley).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Perform insect bioassays with EVG016 and EVG017g.

evidence.quote_or_span: Due to the reduced sporulation of EVG017, not enough spores could be directly harvested for insect bioassays. Therefore, the strain was passaged once through M. sanguinipes by rubbing the abdomen of host insects on an agar culture of EVG017.

Remarkably, the resulting variant, EVG017g, maintained thermotolerance after passage through the insect and showed increased virulence compared to the non-insect passaged parent strain as measured by $ST_{50}$ .
We isolated a variant of EVG017, named EVG017g, that retained thermotolerance but was as capable of conidiation as wild type (See below).
Two strains, termed EVG016 and EVG017 were isolated from cells cultured in cycles 18 and 22, respectively
Similar phenomena, where cycle duration needed to be increased and temperature stabilized to allow fast growing variants to take over, were also seen in cycles 16 (34.
Similar phenomena, where cycle duration needed to be increased and temperature stabilized to allow fast growing variants to take over, were also seen in cycles 16 (34.6°C) and 20 (38°C).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: EVG016 and EVG017g available for virulence testing.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Thermotolerance in vitro may not translate to in vivo pathogenicity at high temperatures.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Thermotolerant variants will retain pathogenicity but may show altered virulence parameters.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conduct insect bioassays at 28°C and 36°C with M. sanguinipes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: None of the strains caused mortality at 36°C. At 28°C, variants retained pathogenicity but with higher LD50 and lower ST50 compared to wild type. Infected insects moved to 28°C after 36°C exposure were rapidly killed, indicating viability at 36°C.

evidence.quote_or_span: None of the strains were pathogenic or able to cause mortality in hosts at 36°C.

However, when insects infected at 36°C were subsequently placed at 28°C, the hosts were rapidly killed by all three fungal strains, indicating that the wild type and adapted strains remained viable at 36°C, but could not cause pathogenicity and death.

Despite their confirmed thermotolerance, the adapted variants did not show increased virulence at 36–37°C, indicating that the ability to grow in vitro at 36–37°C does not necessarily mean that in vivo growth and pathogenesis will occur.

Assays confirmed that thermotolerant variants were, indeed, entomopathogenic, albeit with complex alterations in virulence parameters such as lethal dose responses ( $LD_{50}$ ) and median survival times ( $ST_{50}$ ).
Two strains, termed EVG016 and EVG017 were isolated from cells cultured in cycles 18 and 22, respectively

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Phenotypic data collected; need to interpret selection dynamics.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Stepwise appearance of thermotolerance may reflect mutational complexity or population frequency changes.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Thermal barriers at 32°C, 36.5°C, and 37.5°C may require multiple or complex mutations; apparent stepwise adaptation could be due to mutant frequency changes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze cycle duration and temperature data to identify barriers.
evolution_trajectory.observation: At 32°C, 36.5°C, and 37.5°C, it took longer for favorable variants to take over, suggesting thermal barriers. The stepwise appearance may reflect changes in mutant frequency rather than discrete mutational steps.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations and need for genomic analysis.

evidence.quote_or_span: the selection for faster growing variants was rapid and took roughly the same amount of time. However, at certain temperatures (32°C, 36.5°C and 37.5°C), it took longer for favorable variants to take over, hence these temperatures were considered as thermal barriers

at certain temperatures (32°C, 36.5°C and 37.5°C), it took longer for favorable variants to take over, hence these temperatures were considered as thermal barriers, perhaps requiring multiple or complex mutations to arise in the population.

5°C and 37.5°C), it took longer for favorable variants to take over, hence these temperatures were considered as thermal barriers, perhaps requiring multiple or complex mutations to arise in the population.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Thermal barriers identified; genetic basis unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Uncertain if thermal barriers are concrete or artifacts; genetic changes unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Genome sequencing is needed to identify mutations; further experiments required to validate barriers.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose future work: genome sequencing and additional evolution experiments.
evolution_trajectory.observation: More experiments are needed to determine if these temperatures represent concrete barriers or are artifacts. Genome sequencing of variants is essential.

evidence.quote_or_span: More experiments are needed to determine if these temperatures represent concrete barriers or if they are an artifact of the peculiar evolutionary pathway taken in our experiment.
It will also be important to compare the genome sequence of the thermotolerant variants with that of wild type to determine the genetic changes involved in the acquisition of thermotolerance.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Limitations acknowledged; overall success of directed evolution needs to be stated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to summarize the achievement and its implications.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The directed evolution experiment successfully produced thermotolerant M. anisopliae variants with complex phenotypes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conclude that directed adaptation was successful and that pathogenicity can be retained or recovered.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Successful directed adaptation of M. anisopliae for thermotolerance using Evolugator™; complex phenotypes in non-selected traits but pathogenicity retained.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Finalize the case with success verification.

evidence.quote_or_span: We report the successful directed adaption of a eukaryotic organism, namely the filamentous fungus, M. anisopliae, for increased thermotolerance using a novel continuous culture machine whose operation is fundamentally different from chemostats and other current methods.

Selected mutants displayed complex phenotypes with respect to non-selected attributes such as conidiation and virulence, although both parameters could be retained or recovered while maintaining the thermotolerant phenotype.

validation_methods: ITS1 and Pr1 sequencing to confirm strain identity
Insect bioassays for pathogenicity and virulence
success_verification.final_conclusion: Successful directed adaptation of M. anisopliae for thermotolerance using the Evolugator™. Selected mutants displayed complex phenotypes in non-selected traits, but pathogenicity was retained or recovered.
limitations: Uncertain if thermal barriers are concrete or artifacts
No increased virulence at high temperatures in vivo
